## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Paces

### practice-access-procedures (₢A-AAC) [rough]

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending — running automation tabtargets on real hardware, discovering gaps.

## Docket
Run the new SSH automation tabtargets against the actual Windows host.

**Mac side:**
1. Run platform constructors per BURH profile — creates `burh.env`, displays `ssh-keygen` commands if pubkey missing
2. User runs `ssh-keygen` manually (copy/paste from constructor output)
3. Re-run constructors — picks up `.pub` files, writes complete `burh.env`
4. `buw-HWsc` — write `~/.ssh/config` entries from all profiles
5. Commit + push (BURH profiles now carry real pubkeys)

**Windows/WSL side:**
Git pull in WSL, then run `buw-HWbs`.

Note: sshd is already configured from manual AccessBase practice (steps 1-7). Running `buw-HWbs` will overwrite `sshd_config` and `authorized_keys` with BURH-derived versions. This validates that automation produces a working result, but replaces manual config. Human decides.

**Verify:**
- `buw-HWvs` per profile — ssh via each alias, confirm correct environment lands
- `jjx_bind` to Windows host — confirm relay works

Agent amends tabtarget code for any issues discovered during practice. This is the first real cross-boundary execution — expect discoveries.

### specify-bus0-buwc-module-and-key-builder (₢A-AAR) [rough]

## Character
Spec writing — adding infrastructure-level definitions to BUS0. Mechanical once patterns are understood from the study phase in ₢A-AAQ.

## Docket

Add two infrastructure-level specifications to BUS0:

**1. BUWC Module Declaration**
Define the Windows Commands module as a category in BUS0, parallel to how BURH Regime is declared. Scope: Windows-specific commands that alter system configuration, executed from WSL via PowerShell. Consumes ZBUHW tinder constants from `buhw_windows.sh`. Module file: `buwc_windows.sh`, CLI: `buwc_cli.sh`, function prefix: `buwc_*`, colophon family: `buw-wc*`.

**2. Key Line Builder Contract (`zburh_build_key_line`)**
Specify the shared key-line builder in `burh_regime.sh`. This is the wire format contract between all key-install commands:
- Marker format: `# BURH:<alias>` suffix — the idempotency tag
- Output variables: `ZBURH_KEY_LINE` (full authorized_keys line), `ZBURH_KEY_MARKER` (grep target)
- Line format with command= routing: `command="<BURH_COMMAND>" <BURH_SSH_PUBKEY> # BURH:<BURH_ALIAS>`
- Line format without routing: `<BURH_SSH_PUBKEY> # BURH:<BURH_ALIAS>`
- Pure function — no I/O, callers handle file operations per platform

This is the shared contract that both `burh_install_key` (Unix) and `buwc_install_key` (Windows) depend on. Changes to the marker format break idempotency across both platforms.

Prerequisite: complete study phase from ₢A-AAQ (same reference documents apply).

### specify-new-buk-commands-in-bus0 (₢A-AAQ) [rough]

## Character
Spec writing — studying three existing concept model documents, then applying the patterns to produce two new BUS0 subdocuments. Mechanical once the study phase is complete.

## Docket

Write BUS0 subdocuments for the three new BUK commands added in ₢A-AAC. One command per include file.

**Phase 1 — Study (read-only):**
Read these documents for patterns:
- RBS0 (`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc`) — how RBK structures command specs with include files
- AXLA (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/AXLA-Lexicon.adoc`) — motif vocabulary and voicing annotations
- MCM (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc`) — linked term structure: mapping section, anchors, definition sites
- BUSD-DispatchRuntime.adoc — existing BUS0 subdocument precedent

**Phase 2 — Write three subdocuments:**

Architecture context (resolved in ₢A-AAC): `zburh_build_key_line` in `burh_regime.sh` is the shared pure string builder (sets `ZBURH_KEY_LINE` and `ZBURH_KEY_MARKER`). Platform wrappers handle I/O: Unix file ops on Linux/Mac, PowerShell on Windows. Marker format `# BURH:<alias>` is the idempotency wire contract.

Three commands to specify:
1. `burh_install_key` (buw-rhk) — Linux/Mac key install. Uses `${HOME}/.ssh/authorized_keys`, chmod 600.
2. `buwc_install_key` (buw-wck) — Windows admin key install. Uses `ZBUHW_ADMIN_AUTH_KEYS` (PowerShell I/O), icacls for permissions.
3. `buwc_bootstrap_sshd` (buw-wcb) — Windows sshd provisioning. Platform gate (WSL required), `buc_require "OVERWRITE"` confirmation gate. Owns sshd_config, service, firewall. Does NOT touch authorized_keys.

Each subdocument should specify:
- Inputs (regime variables consumed, folio requirements)
- Preconditions (platform gates, confirmation gates)
- Behavior (what it writes, idempotency guarantees)
- Ownership boundary (what it does and does NOT manage)
- Wire formats (marker pattern for key install commands)

Add `include::` lines to BUS0 at appropriate positions.

### practice-environment-procedures (₢A-AAD) [rough]

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket
User executes Environment procedures on Windows host:
- `buw-HWew` — WSL distro creation (`rbtww-main`)
- `buw-HWec` — Cygwin install

Agent amends handbook for issues. Goal: WSL distro and Cygwin both functional, ready for fundus provisioning and Docker.

### practice-fundus-provisioning (₢A-AAE) [rough]

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket
User runs existing JJK fundus provisioning inside the WSL distro:
- `jjw-tfP1.ProvisionPhase1.sh` (sudo, inside WSL) — create `jjfu_*` accounts
- `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.sh` — clone repos, install BUK

No new code expected — this validates that the existing `jjfp_fundus.sh` works unchanged inside WSL on Windows. If issues arise (platform detection, path differences, SSH localhost behavior in WSL), agent amends `jjfp_fundus.sh` or documents WSL-specific caveats.

### practice-docker-procedures (₢A-AAF) [rough]

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket
User executes Docker procedures on Windows host:
- `rbw-HWdd` — Docker Desktop install + WSL integration
- `rbw-HWdw` — native dockerd inside WSL distro
- `rbw-HWdc` — docker context discipline (WSL uses native, Windows/Cygwin use Desktop)

Agent amends handbook for issues. Goal: both Docker daemons running, context routing deterministic, `jjfu_*` users can access Docker inside WSL.

### create-verification-tabtargets (₢A-AAH) [rough]

## Character
Design + implementation — informed by practice walkthrough discoveries.

## Docket
Create automation tabtargets that probe Windows host health over SSH. SSH connectivity verification moved to ₢A-AAL (`buw-HWvs`); this pace covers post-SSH infrastructure health.

Candidates (refine based on what practice paces reveal):
- WSL distro health: systemd running, expected packages present
- Fundus accounts: `jjfu_*` users exist and are SSH-reachable
- Docker dual-daemon: both daemons running, context routing correct
- End-to-end: RBK test fixture can charge/quench inside WSL

These are automation tabtargets (exit status matters), not handbooks. They run from the curia and probe the Windows host via SSH — so they depend on ₢A-AAC (practice-access) completing successfully.

Scope will narrow during practice — not all candidates may be worth automating.

### reconsider-cygwin-install-procedure (₢A-AAI) [rough]

## Character
Design review — lightweight judgment call.

## Docket
Revisit whether `buw-HWec` should dictate full Cygwin installation or just configure an existing install. After practicing the other procedures, we'll have better intuition about how much hand-holding the handbook should provide vs assuming prerequisites are already in place.

### adopt-step-autonumber-across-all-procedures (₢A-AAJ) [rough]

## Character
Mechanical sweep with judgment — convert existing procedures, evaluate whether the pattern held up.

## Docket
After practicing the Windows procedures with `buh_step1`/`buh_step2`, decide whether to adopt auto-numbered steps across all handbook procedures (onboarding, payor). Convert if yes, document why not if no. Includes PayorEstablish, PayorRefresh, QuotaBuild, and any onboarding procedures still using hardcoded `buh_section "N. ..."`.

### retest-fundus-after-practice (₢A-AAP) [rough]

## Character
Test execution — regression check after practice-driven amendments.

## Docket
Re-run the full fundus scenario test suite (`tt/jjw-tfs.TestFundusScenario.localhost.sh`) to confirm that any code changes made during practice paces (AAC–AAF) haven't broken the BURH-based bind path.

Same coverage as ₢A-AAO but exercised after practice amendments have landed. If failures surface, fix and iterate.

### handbook-render-param1-coverage (₢A-AAS) [rough]

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢A-AAT) [rough]

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Goal: run RB's container isolation tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons. Setup splits into automation tabtargets (SSH keys, sshd config, authorized_keys, connectivity verification) and handbook residue for irreducibly manual steps (WSL install, Cygwin install, Docker Desktop install).
6	
7	Raw draft captured in `Memos/memo-20260412-windows-handbook-draft.md` (9 procedures from an earlier agent session, uses placeholder names and wrong prefix `RBWH`).
8	
9	## Design Decisions
10	
11	**Prefix**: `RBHW` (child of `rbh` handbook family). Draft used `RBWH` which conflicts with `rbw` (workbench terminal exclusivity).
12	
13	**Handbook colophon family** (`rbw-h`):
14	
15	The `rbh` family has three real groups: onboarding, payor, windows. Speculative slots (governor, director, retriever) dropped — those are walkthrough steps within onboarding, not standalone groups.
16	
17	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Role |
18	|----------|-------------|------|
19	| `rbw-h0` | HandbookTOP | Top-level index (all groups) |
20	| `rbw-ho` | HandbookOnboarding | Onboarding group top |
21	| `rbw-hp` | HandbookPayor | Payor group top |
22	| `rbw-hw` | HandbookWindows | Windows group top |
23	| `rbw-HO*` | — | Onboarding subordinate procedures |
24	| `rbw-HP*` | — | Payor subordinate procedures |
25	| `rbw-HW*` | — | Windows subordinate procedures |
26	
27	`0` sentinel sorts first in `ls tt/rbw-h*`, group tops (lowercase) sort next, subordinates (uppercase) follow. Natural reading order.
28	
29	**Three-kit split** — divided by mechanism vs policy:
30	
31	| Kit | Scope | Tabtargets |
32	|-----|-------|------------|
33	| BUK | Generic OS mechanisms + BURH regime | 6 constructors (`buw-rhc*` in `burh_cli.sh`), 3 automation (`buw-HW*`), 6 handbooks (`buw-HW*`) |
34	| JJK (existing `jjw-tfP1/P2`) | Fundus user provisioning (`jjfu_*` accounts) | 0 new — existing P1/P2 already work inside WSL |
35	| RBK (`rbw-HW*`) | Project topology + Docker policy | 4: DockerDesktop, DockerWSLNative, DockerContextDiscipline, orchestrator |
36	
37	**Key insight**: JJK's `jjfp_fundus.sh` already handles Linux account provisioning (create `jjfu_*` users, install SSH keys, clone repos). It runs unchanged inside a WSL distro. The draft's `alice`/`bob` users were always `jjfu_*` profiles wearing placeholder names.
38	
39	**BURH — BUK Regime Host** (new regime, discovered during AccessBase practice):
40	
41	Per-user, per-connection-profile regime for SSH access to remote hosts. Solves three problems discovered during first practice:
42	1. AccessRemote took 4 free-form params that the user had to guess
43	2. No way to transport public key material between machines except manual transcription
44	3. Connective details (host IP, username, key names, aliases) scattered across human memory
45	
46	Directory structure:
47	```
48	.buk/users/${BURS_USER}/<virtual-hostname>/burh.env
49	```
50	
51	Each profile is one SSH connection — one key, one alias, one entry point. Three profiles for Windows (cygwin, wsl, ps) share the same physical host but are distinct connections.
52	
53	Schema (5 fields):
54	```bash
55	BURH_HOST=192.168.86.27
56	BURH_USER=bhyslop
57	BURH_ALIAS=winhost-cyg
58	BURH_SSH_PUBKEY='ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAA... bhyslop@macmini'
59	BURH_COMMAND='C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l'
60	```
61	
62	- `BURH_HOST` — IP or hostname of the physical machine
63	- `BURH_USER` — username on the remote (may differ from local `BURS_USER`)
64	- `BURH_ALIAS` — SSH alias and key filename (must match directory name)
65	- `BURH_SSH_PUBKEY` — full public key line (git-transported, solves chicken-and-egg)
66	- `BURH_COMMAND` — shell command for `command=` routing in `authorized_keys`
67	
68	Single-quoted values for Windows backslashes. Git-safe: no private key material.
69	
70	`BURS_USER` addition: station regime gets a new field identifying the local developer. Routes to the correct `.buk/users/` subdirectory.
71	
72	**Architectural pivot — handbooks → automation with handbook residue:**
73	
74	BURH profiles make most SSH setup automatable. Rather than handbooks that display commands for humans to copy, tabtargets execute directly. Two-phase connectivity model:
75	
76	| Phase | Boundary | How tabtargets run |
77	|-------|----------|-------------------|
78	| Bootstrap | No SSH yet — human at Windows console, WSL available (the feature, not a specific distro) | Run tabtargets directly in WSL terminal |
79	| Operational | SSH works | Relay from Mac via `jjx_bind`/`jjx_relay` |
80	
81	The bootstrap tabtarget's job is to get from phase 1 to phase 2.
82	
83	**PowerShell-from-WSL pattern** (BCG-compliant):
84	
85	`powershell.exe` is callable from WSL bash as a plain external command. No subshells needed — BCG's standard temp-file capture and `|| buc_die` patterns apply directly:
86	```bash
87	powershell.exe -Command "Get-Service sshd" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed"
88	```
89	Bash owns control flow, error handling, and output. PowerShell is just the syscall layer for Windows operations. Complex PowerShell avoided entirely — one atomic command per call.
90	
91	**Impact — handbook procedures absorbed into tabtargets:**
92	- `buw-HWab` (AccessBase) → absorbed into `buw-HWbs` (BootstrapSshd) steps 1-6
93	- `buw-HWar` (AccessRemote) → absorbed into platform constructors + `buw-HWsc` (SshConfig). Key generation is manual — constructors display the `ssh-keygen` command, user runs it, re-runs constructor to populate pubkey. No tabtarget writes to `~/.ssh/`.
94	- `buw-HWax` (AccessEntrypoints) → absorbed into `buw-HWbs` step 4 (authorized_keys from BURH)
95	- Original handbook files become thin wrappers: "run this tabtarget" + context for remaining manual steps
96	
97	**BURH profile constructors** — one per target platform:
98	
99	| Constructor | Alias suffix | BURH_COMMAND | Notes |
100	|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
101	| Linux | `-linux` | empty | Remote Linux, default shell |
102	| macOS | `-mac` | empty | Remote Mac, default shell |
103	| Cygwin | `-cyg` | `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l` | Windows Cygwin |
104	| WSL | `-wsl` | `C:\...\wsl.exe -d {DISTRO_CONST} ...` | WSL distro name from kindle constant |
105	| PowerShell | `-ps` | `C:\...\powershell.exe` | Windows PowerShell |
106	| Localhost | special | empty | `host=localhost`, no command= routing |
107	
108	Common params: `host`, `user`, `moniker`. Alias = `{moniker}-{suffix}`. `BURH_COMMAND` empty is valid (validation min-length changed to 0). Constructors read `~/.ssh/{alias}.pub` if present; otherwise display keygen command for user to run manually. Constructor implementations live in `burh_cli.sh` (command layer), not `burh_regime.sh` (read-only kindle/enforce). Colophon family: `buw-rhc*` (children of existing `buw-rh` host regime ops).
109	
110	**Step auto-numbering** (`buh_step1`/`buh_step2`):
111	
112	Discovered during AccessBase practice that hardcoded step numbers cause renumbering bugs on every insertion/deletion. Implemented mutable kindle state (`z_buh_step_n`, `z_buh_substep_n`, `z_buh_body_indent`) following the zipper roll precedent for controlled mutables. `buh_section` resets indent to top level. All 8 Windows/Docker handbook procedures converted.
113	
114	**sshd_config discovery** (from AccessBase practice — now baked into `buw-HWbs` template):
115	- `UsePAM` and `ChallengeResponseAuthentication` are unsupported by Windows OpenSSH — service fails to start with any unrecognized directive
116	- `buw-HWbs` writes sshd_config from a known-good template (incorporating these constraints), then validates with `sshd -t` before applying
117	- The original handbook's copy-edit-validate-replace workflow is superseded by template-write
118	
119	**Docker stays in RBK** because the dual-daemon topology (Desktop for Windows/Cygwin, native for WSL) is a Recipe Bottle testing decision, not generic Windows setup.
120	
121	**Furnish weight**: BUK handbook CLI needs only `buh_*` combinators — thin furnish like `rbho_cli.sh`. RBK orchestrator needs BUK constants plus its own Docker constants — still thin, no regime/OAuth/IAM.
122	
123	**Style rule**: Use `buh_*` combinators exclusively. The `rbhp_establish` function is the template (new style). Do NOT follow `rbhp_refresh` or `rbhp_quota_build`'s old-style `zrbhp_show()` private color variables.

125	**Handbook/tabtarget separation**: Handbooks display, tabtargets do — but BURH sharpens this: most "display" handbooks become "run this tabtarget" wrappers. The `rbho_onboarding.sh` pattern still holds for irreducibly manual steps (GUI installs).
126	
127	**Verification tabtargets**: SSH verify (`buw-HWvs`) built in ₢A-AAL. Post-SSH health probes (WSL, fundus, Docker) deferred to ₢A-AAH after practice walkthroughs.
128	
129	**Fundus capability registry**: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md` — agent-interpreted inventory of all test targets. Prototype, not program-readable. TBD fields fill in as practice paces complete.

367	`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

369	### Concrete trigger
370	Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
371	```bash
372	buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
373	```
374	`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.
375	
376	The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

378	### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
379	`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.
380	
381	### Candidate directions
382	- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
383	- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
384	- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
385	- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.
386	
387	### Prerequisites
388	- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
389	- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

393	- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
394	- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
395	- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

399	- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Context

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Goal: run RB's container isolation tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons. Setup splits into automation tabtargets (SSH keys, sshd config, authorized_keys, connectivity verification) and handbook residue for irreducibly manual steps (WSL install, Cygwin install, Docker Desktop install).

Raw draft captured in `Memos/memo-20260412-windows-handbook-draft.md` (9 procedures from an earlier agent session, uses placeholder names and wrong prefix `RBWH`).

## Design Decisions

**Prefix**: `RBHW` (child of `rbh` handbook family). Draft used `RBWH` which conflicts with `rbw` (workbench terminal exclusivity).

**Handbook colophon family** (`rbw-h`):

The `rbh` family has three real groups: onboarding, payor, windows. Speculative slots (governor, director, retriever) dropped — those are walkthrough steps within onboarding, not standalone groups.

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Role |
|----------|-------------|------|
| `rbw-h0` | HandbookTOP | Top-level index (all groups) |
| `rbw-ho` | HandbookOnboarding | Onboarding group top |
| `rbw-hp` | HandbookPayor | Payor group top |
| `rbw-hw` | HandbookWindows | Windows group top |
| `rbw-HO*` | — | Onboarding subordinate procedures |
| `rbw-HP*` | — | Payor subordinate procedures |
| `rbw-HW*` | — | Windows subordinate procedures |

`0` sentinel sorts first in `ls tt/rbw-h*`, group tops (lowercase) sort next, subordinates (uppercase) follow. Natural reading order.

**Three-kit split** — divided by mechanism vs policy:

| Kit | Scope | Tabtargets |
|-----|-------|------------|
| BUK | Generic OS mechanisms + BURH regime | 6 constructors (`buw-rhc*` in `burh_cli.sh`), 3 automation (`buw-HW*`), 6 handbooks (`buw-HW*`) |
| JJK (existing `jjw-tfP1/P2`) | Fundus user provisioning (`jjfu_*` accounts) | 0 new — existing P1/P2 already work inside WSL |
| RBK (`rbw-HW*`) | Project topology + Docker policy | 4: DockerDesktop, DockerWSLNative, DockerContextDiscipline, orchestrator |

**Key insight**: JJK's `jjfp_fundus.sh` already handles Linux account provisioning (create `jjfu_*` users, install SSH keys, clone repos). It runs unchanged inside a WSL distro. The draft's `alice`/`bob` users were always `jjfu_*` profiles wearing placeholder names.

**BURH — BUK Regime Host** (new regime, discovered during AccessBase practice):

Per-user, per-connection-profile regime for SSH access to remote hosts. Solves three problems discovered during first practice:
1. AccessRemote took 4 free-form params that the user had to guess
2. No way to transport public key material between machines except manual transcription
3. Connective details (host IP, username, key names, aliases) scattered across human memory

Directory structure:
```
.buk/users/${BURS_USER}/<virtual-hostname>/burh.env
```

Each profile is one SSH connection — one key, one alias, one entry point. Three profiles for Windows (cygwin, wsl, ps) share the same physical host but are distinct connections.

Schema (5 fields):
```bash
BURH_HOST=192.168.86.27
BURH_USER=bhyslop
BURH_ALIAS=winhost-cyg
BURH_SSH_PUBKEY='ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAA... bhyslop@macmini'
BURH_COMMAND='C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l'
```

- `BURH_HOST` — IP or hostname of the physical machine
- `BURH_USER` — username on the remote (may differ from local `BURS_USER`)
- `BURH_ALIAS` — SSH alias and key filename (must match directory name)
- `BURH_SSH_PUBKEY` — full public key line (git-transported, solves chicken-and-egg)
- `BURH_COMMAND` — shell command for `command=` routing in `authorized_keys`

Single-quoted values for Windows backslashes. Git-safe: no private key material.

`BURS_USER` addition: station regime gets a new field identifying the local developer. Routes to the correct `.buk/users/` subdirectory.

**Architectural pivot — handbooks → automation with handbook residue:**

BURH profiles make most SSH setup automatable. Rather than handbooks that display commands for humans to copy, tabtargets execute directly. Two-phase connectivity model:

| Phase | Boundary | How tabtargets run |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| Bootstrap | No SSH yet — human at Windows console, WSL available (the feature, not a specific distro) | Run tabtargets directly in WSL terminal |
| Operational | SSH works | Relay from Mac via `jjx_bind`/`jjx_relay` |

The bootstrap tabtarget's job is to get from phase 1 to phase 2.

**PowerShell-from-WSL pattern** (BCG-compliant):

`powershell.exe` is callable from WSL bash as a plain external command. No subshells needed — BCG's standard temp-file capture and `|| buc_die` patterns apply directly:
```bash
powershell.exe -Command "Get-Service sshd" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed"
```
Bash owns control flow, error handling, and output. PowerShell is just the syscall layer for Windows operations. Complex PowerShell avoided entirely — one atomic command per call.

**Impact — handbook procedures absorbed into tabtargets:**
- `buw-HWab` (AccessBase) → absorbed into `buw-HWbs` (BootstrapSshd) steps 1-6
- `buw-HWar` (AccessRemote) → absorbed into platform constructors + `buw-HWsc` (SshConfig). Key generation is manual — constructors display the `ssh-keygen` command, user runs it, re-runs constructor to populate pubkey. No tabtarget writes to `~/.ssh/`.
- `buw-HWax` (AccessEntrypoints) → absorbed into `buw-HWbs` step 4 (authorized_keys from BURH)
- Original handbook files become thin wrappers: "run this tabtarget" + context for remaining manual steps

**BURH profile constructors** — one per target platform:

| Constructor | Alias suffix | BURH_COMMAND | Notes |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| Linux | `-linux` | empty | Remote Linux, default shell |
| macOS | `-mac` | empty | Remote Mac, default shell |
| Cygwin | `-cyg` | `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l` | Windows Cygwin |
| WSL | `-wsl` | `C:\...\wsl.exe -d {DISTRO_CONST} ...` | WSL distro name from kindle constant |
| PowerShell | `-ps` | `C:\...\powershell.exe` | Windows PowerShell |
| Localhost | special | empty | `host=localhost`, no command= routing |

Common params: `host`, `user`, `moniker`. Alias = `{moniker}-{suffix}`. `BURH_COMMAND` empty is valid (validation min-length changed to 0). Constructors read `~/.ssh/{alias}.pub` if present; otherwise display keygen command for user to run manually. Constructor implementations live in `burh_cli.sh` (command layer), not `burh_regime.sh` (read-only kindle/enforce). Colophon family: `buw-rhc*` (children of existing `buw-rh` host regime ops).

**Step auto-numbering** (`buh_step1`/`buh_step2`):

Discovered during AccessBase practice that hardcoded step numbers cause renumbering bugs on every insertion/deletion. Implemented mutable kindle state (`z_buh_step_n`, `z_buh_substep_n`, `z_buh_body_indent`) following the zipper roll precedent for controlled mutables. `buh_section` resets indent to top level. All 8 Windows/Docker handbook procedures converted.

**sshd_config discovery** (from AccessBase practice — now baked into `buw-HWbs` template):
- `UsePAM` and `ChallengeResponseAuthentication` are unsupported by Windows OpenSSH — service fails to start with any unrecognized directive
- `buw-HWbs` writes sshd_config from a known-good template (incorporating these constraints), then validates with `sshd -t` before applying
- The original handbook's copy-edit-validate-replace workflow is superseded by template-write

**Docker stays in RBK** because the dual-daemon topology (Desktop for Windows/Cygwin, native for WSL) is a Recipe Bottle testing decision, not generic Windows setup.

**Furnish weight**: BUK handbook CLI needs only `buh_*` combinators — thin furnish like `rbho_cli.sh`. RBK orchestrator needs BUK constants plus its own Docker constants — still thin, no regime/OAuth/IAM.

**Style rule**: Use `buh_*` combinators exclusively. The `rbhp_establish` function is the template (new style). Do NOT follow `rbhp_refresh` or `rbhp_quota_build`'s old-style `zrbhp_show()` private color variables.

**Handbook/tabtarget separation**: Handbooks display, tabtargets do — but BURH sharpens this: most "display" handbooks become "run this tabtarget" wrappers. The `rbho_onboarding.sh` pattern still holds for irreducibly manual steps (GUI installs).

**Verification tabtargets**: SSH verify (`buw-HWvs`) built in ₢A-AAL. Post-SSH health probes (WSL, fundus, Docker) deferred to ₢A-AAH after practice walkthroughs.

**Fundus capability registry**: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md` — agent-interpreted inventory of all test targets. Prototype, not program-readable. TBD fields fill in as practice paces complete.

## Tabtarget Inventory

```
BUK — BURH Constructors (new, from ₢A-AAL — regime ops in burh_cli.sh):
  tt/buw-rhcl.ConstructLinux.sh          — BURH constructor: Linux target
  tt/buw-rhcm.ConstructMac.sh           — BURH constructor: macOS target
  tt/buw-rhcc.ConstructCygwin.sh         — BURH constructor: Windows Cygwin
  tt/buw-rhcw.ConstructWSL.sh           — BURH constructor: Windows WSL
  tt/buw-rhcp.ConstructPowerShell.sh     — BURH constructor: Windows PowerShell
  tt/buw-rhcx.ConstructLocalhost.sh      — BURH constructor: localhost (host=localhost, no command=)

BUK — Automation (new, from ₢A-AAL):
  tt/buw-HWsc.SshConfig.sh              — kindle all BURH profiles, write ~/.ssh/config
  tt/buw-HWvs.VerifySsh.sh              — kindle BURH, ssh test
  tt/buw-HWbs.BootstrapSshd.sh          — idempotent sshd setup via PowerShell (WSL-side)

BUK — Handbooks (existing, becoming thin wrappers):
  tt/buw-hw.HandbookWindows.sh           — BUK-level top checklist (generic OS procedures)
  tt/buw-HWab.AccessBase.sh             — handbook residue: context for buw-HWbs
  tt/buw-HWar.AccessRemote.sh           — handbook residue: context for constructors + buw-HWsc
  tt/buw-HWax.AccessEntrypoints.sh      — handbook residue: context for buw-HWbs step 4
  tt/buw-HWew.EnvironmentWSL.sh         — WSL distro creation (param: distro-name)
  tt/buw-HWec.EnvironmentCygwin.sh      — Cygwin install (verification: bash >= 3.2)

RBK:
  tt/rbw-h0.HandbookTOP.sh              — top-level index across all handbook groups
  tt/rbw-hw.HandbookWindows.sh          — orchestrator referencing BUK + JJK + RBK steps via buh_T
  tt/rbw-HWdd.DockerDesktop.sh          — Docker Desktop install
  tt/rbw-HWdw.DockerWSLNative.sh        — native dockerd in WSL (param: distro-name)
  tt/rbw-HWdc.DockerContextDiscipline.sh — deterministic daemon selection
```

## Open Questions
- `rbtww-main` mint deconfliction deferred to post-MVP
- BUK handbook module naming: `buhw_*` functions in `buhw_windows.sh` (new file, not extending `buh_handbook.sh`)
- Handbook family reorganization (renaming existing `rbw-go*`/`rbw-gP*` to `rbw-HO*`/`rbw-HP*`) is a separate heat or pace in `₣A6` (handbook-restart)

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAC

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending — running automation tabtargets on real hardware, discovering gaps.

## Docket
Run the new SSH automation tabtargets against the actual Windows host.

**Mac side:**
1. Run platform constructors per BURH profile — creates `burh.env`, displays `ssh-keygen` commands if pubkey missing
2. User runs `ssh-keygen` manually (copy/paste from constructor output)
3. Re-run constructors — picks up `.pub` files, writes complete `burh.env`
4. `buw-HWsc` — write `~/.ssh/config` entries from all profiles
5. Commit + push (BURH profiles now carry real pubkeys)

**Windows/WSL side:**
Git pull in WSL, then run `buw-HWbs`.

Note: sshd is already configured from manual AccessBase practice (steps 1-7). Running `buw-HWbs` will overwrite `sshd_config` and `authorized_keys` with BURH-derived versions. This validates that automation produces a working result, but replaces manual config. Human decides.

**Verify:**
- `buw-HWvs` per profile — ssh via each alias, confirm correct environment lands
- `jjx_bind` to Windows host — confirm relay works

Agent amends tabtarget code for any issues discovered during practice. This is the first real cross-boundary execution — expect discoveries.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAD

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket
User executes Environment procedures on Windows host:
- `buw-HWew` — WSL distro creation (`rbtww-main`)
- `buw-HWec` — Cygwin install

Agent amends handbook for issues. Goal: WSL distro and Cygwin both functional, ready for fundus provisioning and Docker.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAE

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket
User runs existing JJK fundus provisioning inside the WSL distro:
- `jjw-tfP1.ProvisionPhase1.sh` (sudo, inside WSL) — create `jjfu_*` accounts
- `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.sh` — clone repos, install BUK

## Character

## Docket

Write BUS0 subdocuments for the three new BUK commands added in ₢A-AAC. One command per include file.

**Phase 1 — Study (read-only):**
Read these documents for patterns:
- RBS0 (`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc`) — how RBK structures command specs with include files
- AXLA (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/AXLA-Lexicon.adoc`) — motif vocabulary and voicing annotations
- MCM (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc`) — linked term structure: mapping section, anchors, definition sites
- BUSD-DispatchRuntime.adoc — existing BUS0 subdocument precedent

**Phase 2 — Write three subdocuments:**

Architecture context (resolved in ₢A-AAC): `zburh_build_key_line` in `burh_regime.sh` is the shared pure string builder (sets `ZBURH_KEY_LINE` and `ZBURH_KEY_MARKER`). Platform wrappers handle I/O: Unix file ops on Linux/Mac, PowerShell on Windows. Marker format `# BURH:<alias>` is the idempotency wire contract.

Three commands to specify:
1. `burh_install_key` (buw-rhk) — Linux/Mac key install. Uses `${HOME}/.ssh/authorized_keys`, chmod 600.
2. `buwc_install_key` (buw-wck) — Windows admin key install. Uses `ZBUHW_ADMIN_AUTH_KEYS` (PowerShell I/O), icacls for permissions.
3. `buwc_bootstrap_sshd` (buw-wcb) — Windows sshd provisioning. Platform gate (WSL required), `buc_require "OVERWRITE"` confirmation gate. Owns sshd_config, service, firewall. Does NOT touch authorized_keys.

Each subdocument should specify:
- Inputs (regime variables consumed, folio requirements)
- Preconditions (platform gates, confirmation gates)
- Behavior (what it writes, idempotency guarantees)
- Ownership boundary (what it does and does NOT manage)
- Wire formats (marker pattern for key install commands)

Add `include::` lines to BUS0 at appropriate positions.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAR

## Character
Spec writing — adding infrastructure-level definitions to BUS0. Mechanical once patterns are understood from the study phase in ₢A-AAQ.

## Docket

Add two infrastructure-level specifications to BUS0:

**1. BUWC Module Declaration**
Define the Windows Commands module as a category in BUS0, parallel to how BURH Regime is declared. Scope: Windows-specific commands that alter system configuration, executed from WSL via PowerShell. Consumes ZBUHW tinder constants from `buhw_windows.sh`. Module file: `buwc_windows.sh`, CLI: `buwc_cli.sh`, function prefix: `buwc_*`, colophon family: `buw-wc*`.

**2. Key Line Builder Contract (`zburh_build_key_line`)**
Specify the shared key-line builder in `burh_regime.sh`. This is the wire format contract between all key-install commands:
- Marker format: `# BURH:<alias>` suffix — the idempotency tag
- Output variables: `ZBURH_KEY_LINE` (full authorized_keys line), `ZBURH_KEY_MARKER` (grep target)
- Line format with command= routing: `command="<BURH_COMMAND>" <BURH_SSH_PUBKEY> # BURH:<BURH_ALIAS>`
- Line format without routing: `<BURH_SSH_PUBKEY> # BURH:<BURH_ALIAS>`
- Pure function — no I/O, callers handle file operations per platform

This is the shared contract that both `burh_install_key` (Unix) and `buwc_install_key` (Windows) depend on. Changes to the marker format break idempotency across both platforms.

Prerequisite: complete study phase from ₢A-AAQ (same reference documents apply).

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAS

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAT

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

Here's where ₣A- (`rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures`) stands.

## Heat at a glance
**Status:** racing · **Created:** 260412 · **12 paces remaining**

Goal: get RB's container isolation tests running on a Windows host (WSL + Cygwin + dual Docker daemons). The big architectural work landed in K/L/M/N/O — BURH regime (BUK Regime Host), wired handbooks, JJK BURH bind, validated fundus scenarios. **The pivot was: handbooks → automation tabtargets, with handbook residue only for irreducibly manual steps (GUI installs, ssh-keygen).**

## Paddock highlights
- **BURH** is the new per-user, per-connection-profile regime at `.buk/users/${BURS_USER}/<virtual-hostname>/burh.env`. 5 fields, git-safe (no private keys). Six platform constructors (`buw-rhc{l,m,c,w,p,x}`) emit the profiles.
- **Three-kit split:** BUK owns mechanism + BURH (constructors, automation, residue handbooks), JJK keeps existing fundus provisioning, RBK owns Docker policy.
- **Bootstrap → operational SSH** is the connectivity model: phase-1 tabtargets run directly in WSL terminal; phase-2 tabtargets relay from Mac via `jjx_bind`/`jjx_relay`.
- **Step auto-numbering** (`buh_step1`/`buh_step2`) implemented; converting older procedures is its own pace (AAJ).
- Open Q's still live: `rbtww-main` mint, handbook family reorg.

## Remaining paces (in order)

| Coronet | Silks | Character |
|---|---|---|
| **₢A-AAC** | practice-access-procedures | Human-driven, agent amends — first real cross-boundary run |
| ₢A-AAR | specify-bus0-buwc-module-and-key-builder | Spec writing |
| ₢A-AAQ | specify-new-buk-commands-in-bus0 | Spec writing (study phase + 3 subdocs) |
| ₢A-AAD | practice-environment-procedures | Human-driven on Windows host (WSL/Cygwin) |
| ₢A-AAE | practice-fundus-provisioning | Validate `jjfp_fundus.sh` runs unchanged in WSL |

| ₢A-AAP | retest-fundus-after-practice | Regression check post-amendments |
| ₢A-AAS | handbook-render-param1-coverage | Theurge fixture follow-up (param1 channel) |
| ₢A-AAT | handbook-render-imprint-coverage | Sibling — imprint channel + content drift |

## Sequencing observations
- **AAC is the gate.** It's the first pace that exercises the BURH automation against real hardware — almost everything below it (AAD/AAE/AAF/AAH) needs SSH working to the Windows host. AAR/AAQ specs can be written in parallel since they're desk work.

- **AAH scope narrows during practice** — the docket explicitly says candidates depend on what AAC–AAF reveal.

- Should AAR + AAQ be merged or run in parallel before/during AAC? Both are spec writing and share the study phase.
- AAS and AAT both note "may be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge" — same fixture, same gap (channeled colophons meet uniform fixture shape). Merge candidate.
